summary: I lived in Japan for only 18 years, mostly in a countryside setting where life was simple and education was limited. During my childhood, I walked miles to school through snow and experienced a time when civilization was just beginning to spread. My family followed Buddhist traditions, and I have many fond memories of the mountains, rivers, and village life. Despite the hardships, those years shaped my early understanding of faith and gratitude.

My family practiced Buddhism, specifically the Jōdo Shinshū sect, which deeply influenced my upbringing. My father was a devout believer who performed morning and evening rituals, including prayers and offerings. These practices taught me about faith and reverence from a young age, even before I fully understood them. This early exposure to Jōdo Shinshū eventually led me to appreciate spiritual life and later influenced my journey toward Christian faith.

I moved to a new school in the town of Shiromachi when it was built closer to my village, which made attending school easier. There, I graduated and even entered a special class preparing for advanced studies, learning subjects like English, algebra, and history. Shiromachi represented a significant step in my education during a time when schools were scarce and far apart in rural Japan.

After finishing school, I applied to agricultural schools in Okayama Prefecture but was unable to pass the physical examinations required for admission. This disappointment was part of what motivated me to consider emigrating to the United States. Although I never studied there, Okayama Prefecture was part of my early aspirations for further education.

I also applied to Teacher's College in Hiroshima Prefecture but failed the physical examination, which was a great disappointment to me. This failure, along with others, made me feel my future was bleak and contributed to my decision to leave Japan. Hiroshima Prefecture was thus part of my educational journey, even though I could not attend school there.

Mr. Heitaro Wakabayashi was my teacher and an inspiration who had already emigrated to the United States before me. He wrote 'Iafu Shogyokaigisho no Shoki,' memoirs from the Chamber of Commerce in Los Angeles, which I later learned about. His example and the immigration movement around that time motivated me to take the difficult step of going overseas despite my limited education and experience.

I arrived in the United States in the fall of 1906, after a difficult journey on the ship Koria Maru. Coming from rural Japan, I was amazed by the modern cities like San Francisco with their tall buildings and wide streets. Life was hard at first; I worked in an apple canning factory near Watsonville and struggled with loneliness and language barriers. Despite these challenges, I was determined to learn English and find better opportunities in this new land.

The Koria Maru was the ship that brought me from Japan to the United States in 1906. It was a large vessel of about 12 to 13 thousand tons, and I was placed in the bottom area with the cargo. The journey was difficult, especially as people above me suffered from seasickness, but I remember it vividly as the start of my new life abroad.

When I landed in San Francisco, I was inspected for diseases like Deuddenal worms and eye problems, but the process was smooth and without difficulty. Coming from a rural Japanese village, I was overwhelmed by the city's tall buildings and wide streets, which seemed magnificent and unlike anything I had seen before. San Francisco was my first impression of America and marked the beginning of my challenging but hopeful journey.

The Mikado Hotel was where I stayed when I first arrived in the United States, and the hotel owner helped me find a job in Watsonville at an apple canning factory. The hotel was a base for me as I searched for work and tried to learn English. It was an important place during my early days in America, providing support when I was new and struggling.

I worked in an apple canning factory in Watsonville, California, shortly after arriving in the United States. The job was tough, and the living conditions were basic, with about 20 young men sleeping together on blankets on the floor. It was a lonely and difficult time, and I often thought about my home in Japan with sadness. Watsonville was my first real experience of labor in America and taught me a lot about hardship and perseverance.
